pattern: A family shared: a maternal uncle developed serious mental health problems while he was a Master’s student; he has been on psychiatric medication for many years. Relatives worry about his future as parents age and how the family will support him long term.

A young adult wrote: after heartbreak they felt sad, but their mother kept nagging harshly and cursing; they felt mentally exhausted and suicidal thoughts appeared. (If you are in crisis, contact Nepal Helpline 1166 or local emergency services.)

Poster asked why it is so hard to deal with Nepali parents: they try to honour parents’ wishes and care for them as they age, but feel behind relatives in career after delays and mistakes—and the emotional weight is heavy.

A doctor gave up a full MD scholarship seat in Nepal because of mental health struggles; relatives questioned the decision loudly and offered unsolicited advice—the poster said the last months were the hardest.
Someone wrote that many Nepalis act like everything is fine while struggling inside—you can smile and talk normally even when nothing inside feels okay; stigma hides what is really going on.
A woman described living with manipulative extended family (big mother) for decades: jealousy toward her biological parents, negativity, and stress affecting the whole household’s mental health.
A first-time poster said many things piled up at once—they feel mentally stuck, usually handle problems alone, but personal issues now make it hard to think clearly.
A Nepali in the USA wrote about layoff and interview stress—job loss hit their mental health and they felt pressure to “crack” the final interview by any means.

Someone described a 21-year-old friend taken by parents to a mental hospital who then went silent—background included emotional abuse at home and police involvement; the situation felt unsafe and confusing.
Poster noted TikTok and reels about losing loved ones to depression in Nepal—people rarely feel free to talk about mental health openly, and they wished for better awareness platforms.
A man asked whether Nepali men abroad can really open up to male friends—man-to-man listening and understanding feelings—without judgement.

Someone went through major depression with self-harm thoughts, shut down social media and their phone; their best friend reached out via the poster’s sister because they could not share struggles directly.
A poster’s therapist recommended inpatient care after a crisis, but parents emotionally blackmailed them to stay home instead of being admitted— they did not know what to do.
A community member warned that mental health and rehab centres in Nepal often lack proper regulation—ranging from taking money without care to worse outcomes including abuse.
During political turmoil, volunteers shared links to free online counselling and mental health support so people would not suffer alone.
A 19-year-old described a parent’s accident and police follow-up—fear and family stress piled up fast after a drunk-driver incident, on top of feeling a breakdown.
